A1-QUIZ.title: RFI Fundamentals
A1-QUIZ.description: Test your understanding of opening ranges and position discipline

questions.stem: What is the primary reason to open-raise from early position?
questions.scenario: You hold 77 in UTG (first to act after the big blind)

choices.label: To build the pot with a strong hand
Because many opponents fold
To represent a strong range and deny equity to later positions
To induce action from recreational players

questions.explanation: Opening from early position is about range strength and positional awareness. You're playing from a position disadvantage, so you need a stronger hand to open. The goal is to deny equity to players who will act after you.

questions.stem: Which hand is typically the weakest in your UTG opening range?
questions.scenario: Building a balanced UTG open-raising range

choices.label: A9s
KTo
QQ

questions.explanation: KTo (King-Ten offsuit) is typically the weakest hand in a tight UTG opening range. It has poor playability post-flop and limited straight potential compared to suited connectors or lower pairs.

questions.stem: What size should your standard open-raise be in a 6-max cash game?
questions.scenario: Standard open in a $1/$2 deep cash game

choices.label: 2.5-3x the big blind
4-5x the big blind
1.5x the big blind
Any size is fine as long as you play well

questions.explanation: The standard open-raise in 6-max is 2.5-3x the big blind. This balances between getting value and managing risk. Smaller opens allow more flexibility; larger opens are for specific situations.

questions.stem: When opening from the button, you can relax your range because:

choices.label: You always act last post-flop
Recreational players always fold to button opens
The button is the most profitable position
You can see the flop for free

questions.explanation: Playing from the button means you always act last post-flop, giving you informational and positional advantage. This allows you to open a wider range because you can see how opponents react before making decisions.

A2-QUIZ.title: Opening Ranges and Position
A2-QUIZ.description: Test your understanding of 3-bet ranges and positional adjustments

questions.stem: What is a 'linear' 3-bet range?

choices.label: A range that includes only premium hands (AA-QQ)
A range that goes from medium hands to nuts in a straight line
A range that is perfectly polarized
A range that only includes suited connectors

questions.explanation: A linear 3-bet range includes value hands (AA-QQ, AK) at the top, medium hands (AQ, KQs, JJ) in the middle, and some bluffs at the bottom. It 'lines up' from strongest to weakest.

questions.stem: Against a 3x open from a tight regular, what should influence your 3-bet sizing?
questions.scenario: Deciding on 3-bet sizing against a standard opener

choices.label: Always 3-bet 3x to keep the SPR manageable
Use larger sizes with premium hands, smaller with medium ones
Always 3-bet large (4-5x) to punish their range
Match their size exactly regardless of hand strength

questions.explanation: Your 3-bet sizing should be hand-dependent. Larger sizes with premium hands extract value and protect your range. Smaller sizes with medium hands allow you to continue profitably while managing risk.

questions.stem: In a 3-bet pot, which player typically has the range advantage?

choices.label: The 3-bettor always has the advantage
The caller always has the advantage
The 3-bettor at dry boards, the caller at dynamic boards
Neither - 3-bet pots are always marginal

questions.explanation: The 3-bettor typically has the advantage on dry boards (paired, Ace-high, rainbow) because their range is stronger. On dynamic boards (connected, suited, monboard), the caller can have more made hands and draws, giving them equity.

questions.stem: When facing a 3-bet, which scenario suggests calling rather than 4-betting or folding?
questions.scenario: Deciding between calling, 4-betting, or folding to a 3-bet

choices.label: You hold AA and want to trap
You hold a medium pocket pair and the 3-bettor is aggressive
You hold KJo and the 3-bet is small
You hold A5s and want to realize equity

questions.explanation: Medium pairs often play better as calls against aggressive 3-bettors because 4-betting commits you too deep, and folding discards equity. Calling lets you realize your equity post-flop with position.

A3-QUIZ.title: Three-Betting and Facing Pressure
A3-QUIZ.description: Test your understanding of defense strategies and pressure handling

questions.stem: What is 'bucket' thinking in 3-bet defense?

choices.label: Sorting your hands by suit for better post-flop play
Categorizing hands into fold, call, and 4-bet buckets for quick decisions
Grouping all suited connectors together
Storing your biggest hands for big pots

questions.explanation: Bucket thinking means categorizing your defensive actions into clear buckets: fold (too weak), call (playable but not strong enough to 4-bet), and 4-bet (premium hands). This speeds up decisions and reduces leaks.

questions.stem: Why should you avoid calling with dominated offsuit hands like KJo against a 3-bet?

choices.label: Because you'll always miss the flop
Because your kicker gets dominated by their value range and you're dominated by their bluffs
Because you should always 4-bet with position
Because KJo is too strong to call

questions.explanation: KJo has reverse implied odds against a strong 3-betting range. When you hit a King, opponents with AK, KQ, or KK have you dominated. When you hit a Jack, you often face higher jacks.

questions.stem: When should you 4-bet instead of calling with a premium hand like QQ?
questions.scenario: Deciding between calling and 4-betting with Queens

choices.label: Always 4-bet QQ to build the pot
When the opponent's 3-bet range is wide and they'll fold too much
Never - QQ plays better as a call
Only when you're heads-up against a recreational player

questions.explanation: You should 4-bet with QQ when you expect the opponent to fold often (they have a wide 3-bet range or show weakness). 4-betting extracts value and denies equity to their floating range.

questions.stem: What is the key principle when facing a 3-bet out of position?
questions.scenario: Defending against a 3-bet from the small blind

choices.label: Call more because you have position on the big blind
Narrow your range and play conservatively due to positional disadvantage
Always 4-bet to take control
Float more often since you'll have position post-flop

questions.explanation: When out of position, you should defend more tightly. You're giving up positional advantage and will face difficult post-flop decisions. Narrow your calling range and focus on hands with good post-flop playability.

A4-QUIZ.title: Blind Defense Foundations
A4-QUIZ.description: Test your understanding of blind defense strategy

questions.stem: What is the minimum defense frequency (MDF) concept?

choices.label: The percentage of hands you must defend to prevent being exploited
The optimal number of hands to open-raise from the big blind
The maximum amount you can call with in a 3-bet pot
The minimum bet size needed to fold out opponents

questions.explanation: MDF is the minimum percentage of hands you must continue with when facing a bet to remain unexploitable. It's calculated as: bet / (bet + pot). For a standard 3x open, MDF is approximately 33%.

questions.stem: When defending the big blind, which factor should you prioritize?
questions.scenario: Deciding what matters most in BB defense

choices.label: Pot odds alone
Equity realization and playability post-flop
The opponent's table image
The size of your stack

questions.explanation: While pot odds matter, hands that realize their equity well (suited connectors, small pairs, broadway cards) perform better from the big blind. You must consider how well hands play post-flop, not just pre-flop equity.

questions.stem: What should happen to your calling range when facing an open from a tight player?
questions.scenario: Adjusting BB defense against different opponent types

choices.label: Widen it because they'll fold more
Narrow it because their range is stronger
Keep it the same - ranges are based on position, not opponents
Only defend with premium hands

questions.explanation: When facing a tighter opening range, your defense should tighten. Their range is stronger, so you need stronger hands to continue. Their value hands dominate more of your range.

questions.stem: From the small blind, why is defense more challenging than from the big blind?

choices.label: You have to post the ante
You're out of position for the entire hand and must act first post-flop
The big blind has more information
You can't 3-bet profitably from the small blind

questions.explanation: Defending from the small blind is harder because you're playing out of position for the entire hand. You face a difficult post-flop decision every time you call, making it crucial to defend with hands that play well OOP.

A5-QUIZ.title: Isolation and Punish Spots
A5-QUIZ.description: Test your understanding of isolation strategy and limper punishment

questions.stem: What is the primary goal when isolating a limper?
questions.scenario: Deciding how to play against a preflop limper

choices.label: To get all-in with premium hands
To get heads-up against the limper while denying equity to other opponents
To maximize the pot size regardless of opponent count
To trap recreational players

questions.explanation: The main goal of isolation is to get heads-up against the limper while keeping other players out. More players dilute your equity and make the pot more difficult to play. You want to isolate the weak player.

questions.stem: What size should you use when isolating a recreational player who limps?

choices.label: Standard 3x open
Larger (4-5x) to extract value from recreational players
Smaller (2-2.5x) to induce action
Always match the limper's limp size

questions.explanation: When isolating recreational players, use a larger size. They often have weak, sticky ranges and are unlikely to fold. A larger size extracts more value and compensates for the higher variance of playing against their wider range.

questions.stem: Which hands make the best isolation candidates against a limper?

choices.label: Medium pairs and suited connectors
Broadway cards and premium pairs that have good post-flop playability
Any two cards - isolation is about position, not hand strength
Only AA-KK

questions.explanation: Good isolation hands have equity edge over limper's range and play well post-flop. Broadway cards (ATs, KQs, JTs) and premium pairs (JJ-AA) work well because they have decent equity and can bet/fold on various textures.

questions.stem: When multiple players limp, what should guide your isolation strategy?

choices.label: Always raise to isolate - more players means more value
Only isolate the recreational player in the hand; don't isolate vs regulars
Never isolate multi-way - check instead
Raise small to get all of them to fold

questions.explanation: In multi-way pots, focus on isolating the weakest player (usually recreational). Don't raise to isolate against regulars who defend correctly. Your raise should target the recreational player while being aware that regulars may call.

A6-QUIZ.title: Population Exploits and Adjustments
A6-QUIZ.description: Test your understanding of depth-aware strategy and population adjustments

questions.stem: How does effective stack depth affect hand values preflop?

choices.label: Depth doesn't matter preflop - only post-flop
In shallow games (40-60bb), premiums like AA/QQ are worth more, suited connectors less
In deep games (200bb+), all hands are equally valuable
Stack depth only matters for set-mining

questions.explanation: In shallow games, premiums (AA, KK, QQ) are worth more because you can get stacks in more easily. Suited connectors lose value because you won't realize equity fully - there isn't enough behind to justify the implied odds.

questions.stem: Against a population that overfolds to 3-bets, what is the best exploit?

choices.label: Tighten your 3-bet range
3-bet lighter with more hands to maximize fold equity
Always slowplay your premiums
Check more post-flop

questions.explanation: When the population overfolds to 3-bets, you should 3-bet more hands. This is called 'light 3-betting' and exploits their fold frequency. Even with medium hands, you can 3-bet as a bluff and win the pot preflop.

questions.stem: What is a ' polarized' 4-bet range?

choices.label: A range with only value hands (AA-QQ)
A range with only bluffs
A range with nuts at the top and bluffs at the bottom, nothing in the middle
A range that includes all medium-strength hands

questions.explanation: A polarized 4-bet range contains the strongest hands (AA, KK, sometimes AK) for value and suited connectors or other bluffs at the bottom. Medium hands (QQ, JJ, AQ) typically just call rather than 4-bet.

questions.stem: In deep cash games (300bb+), which hand type gains the most value?

choices.label: High pairs (AA, KK)
Suited connectors and suited aces
Offsuit broadway
Medium pairs

questions.explanation: In deep games, suited connectors and suited aces gain value because you can extract more money when you hit big hands. The implied odds are higher - when you hit a straight or flush, stacks go in more often.

A7-QUIZ.title: Preflop System Integration
A7-QUIZ.description: Test your understanding of building consistent preflop habits

questions.stem: What is the key to building preflop instincts?

choices.label: Memorizing every hand chart exactly
Repetition and consistent review until decisions become automatic
Only playing when you have a strong hand
Avoiding difficult decisions by always folding

questions.explanation: The key to building preflop instincts is repetition. Run drills, review spots, and practice until the decisions become automatic. This frees up mental energy for post-flop play and reduces tilt from preflop spots.

questions.stem: How should you structure your daily preflop practice?

choices.label: Only study when you have losing sessions
15-20 minutes daily of focused repetition, not marathon study sessions
Spend hours memorizing every possible scenario
Watch videos instead of practicing hands

questions.explanation: Daily short sessions (15-20 minutes) are more effective than occasional marathon sessions. Consistent, focused repetition builds muscle memory. The goal is automaticity, not perfection or exhaustive knowledge.

questions.stem: When reviewing your hands, what should you focus on?

choices.label: Only big pots where you got stacked
The decisions that were difficult - those are learning opportunities
Hands where you won money
What the opponent did wrong

questions.explanation: Focus on difficult decisions - those are where the most learning happens. Easy decisions don't need review. When a spot was unclear, figure out the principle and add it to your system. This is how you improve.

questions.stem: What is the ultimate goal of the preflop system?

choices.label: Never lose money preflop
Make preflop automatic so you can focus on post-flop play
Win every hand you play
Make the most aggressive plays possible

questions.explanation: The goal of the preflop system is to make those decisions automatic, freeing mental energy for post-flop play. Poker is complex post-flop, so you want preflop to be a smooth, practiced process, not a constant struggle.
